data.title: Wheat Futures Fall as Black Sea Harvest Progresses — Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais

  • Wheat for September delivery fell 0.4% to $5.06 1/2 a bushel on the Chicago Board of Trade on Friday, declining against this week's strength in corn futures as the Russian harvest nears its completion.
  • Corn for December delivery fell 0.1% to $4.11 a bushel.
  • Soybeans for November delivery rose 0.2% to $10.52 1/2 a bushel.

HIGHLIGHTS

Eyes on Russia: Russia's wheat harvest is nearing completion, with the next step being the Russian crop hitting the export market.

U.S. wheat may be in a better position to compete against Russia on the export market versus previous years, said Charlie Sernatinger of Marex in a note. He adds that U.S. wheat futures may drop for another week before turning around.

"We are still eyeballing the seasonal lows in wheat next Friday," he said.

Swing and a Miss: The EPA published its latest on small refinery exemptions — allowances for blenders of oil-based gasoline to circumvent renewable fuel standards.

More exemptions were allowed than expected by the market, AgResource said. "The exemptions were far larger than expected," the firm said in a note, in response to news that the EPA would allow exemptions for 63 applicants, with partial exemptions granted to more.

But other rumors about the implementation of new rules helped stabilize CBOT grains Friday afternoon.

INSIGHT

Wide Load: Following the close of trading Friday, Pro Farmer released its final assessment of production and yields.

For both corn and soybeans, Pro Farmer said that record yields are coming, with the national corn yield seen at 182.7 bushels an acre and the soybean yield expected at 53 bushels an acre. Both will be record-high yields, if realized.

Pro Farmer added in commentary with its estimates that while strong yield potential is seen in the fields that were surveyed, how the next month proceeds weather-wise is critical.

"This crop has all the potential, and that's what was measured this week," said the trade publication.

Looking for a Cut: At Jackson Hole, Fed Chair Jerome Powell expressed concern about weakening employment, more so than rising inflation. For investors, that means readiness to cut interest rates next month.

Powell was noncommittal and upcoming data could still keep the central bank on hold, but Wall Street mostly overlooked the caveats.

A weaker U.S. dollar is typically supportive for commodities like grains, making U.S. export offerings more attractive to buying nations.

AHEAD

  • The USDA is scheduled to release its weekly grain export inspections report at 11 a.m. EDT Monday.
  • The USDA is due to release its weekly crop progress report at 4 p.m. EDT Monday.
  • The EIA is scheduled to release its weekly ethanol production and stocks report at 10:30 a.m. EDT Wednesday.
  • Paulo Trevisani contributed to this article.

BRIDGFORD FOODS CORP SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Bridgford Foods Corp, a leading producer of frozen and snack food products, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter of fiscal year 2025. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance and operational highlights, reflecting the challenges and strategies in place to navigate the current economic landscape.

Financial Highlights

Net Sales-Consolidated: $51,954 thousand, increased by 5.5% compared to the same period in fiscal year 2024 due to higher selling prices and unit sales volume.

Gross Margin: 20.5%, decreased from 22.8% in the same period in fiscal year 2024, impacted by higher costs of products sold.

Operating Loss: $3,856 thousand, increased from $3,384 thousand in the same period in fiscal year 2024, primarily due to increased SG&A expenses.

Net Loss: $6,610 thousand for the thirty-six weeks ended July 11, 2025, compared to $2,730 thousand in the same period in fiscal year 2024, reflecting higher costs and lower margins.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments: The company operates in two main segments: Frozen Food Products and Snack Food Products. The Frozen Food Products segment includes biscuits, bread dough items, roll dough items, and shelf-stable sandwiches, while the Snack Food Products segment includes dry sausage products, jerky, meat snacks, salami, sausage, and pepperoni products.

Sales Units: In the third quarter of fiscal year 2025, the Snack Food Products segment sold approximately 170 different items through customer-owned distribution centers and a direct-store-delivery network serving approximately 21,000 supermarkets, mass merchandise, and convenience retail stores across all 50 states.

Operational Performance: The Frozen Food Products segment experienced a decrease in net sales due to lower selling prices per pound and lower unit sales volume. Conversely, the Snack Food Products segment saw an increase in net sales driven by higher selling prices per pound and increased unit sales volume.

Future Outlook: The company is focused on restoring profitability by driving top-line revenue growth and reducing costs. It is implementing price increases on products to offset higher costs for meat commodities and is focused on reducing selling, general, and administrative expenses.

Operational Challenges: The company has been impacted by inflation, including higher costs for labor, freight, and specific materials related to product manufacturing and delivery. It expects this trend to continue throughout the remainder of fiscal year 2025.

Production Adjustments: The company has shifted away from company-leased long-haul vehicles toward less costly transportation methods such as common carriers for its Frozen Food Products segment.

Customer Demand Trends: There is a noted shift in consumer spending habits toward purchasing less expensive private-label snack foods, including meat products, to reduce expenses.

Supply Chain Management: The company is seeking bids for its production materials to drive increased competition among its vendors while maintaining quality inputs at the best possible price.

Market Conditions: The company is experiencing fluctuations in commodity costs due to political and economic conditions, including ongoing conflicts and increased tariffs, which may affect its financial results and liquidity.

data.title: Sona poised to hire Cheyne's Hill as CFO for $13.6 billion manager, source says
data.description:
Sona Asset Management is set to hire David Hill as its new chief financial officer, a source close to the matter told Reuters on Friday.

Hill will likely join the investment firm from his CFO role at the $13.5 hedge fund Cheyne Capital, the person said. He brings over 20 years of experience, including 12 years in CFO roles, said the source.

Sona Asset Management's chief financial officer Iain Colquhoun has left the firm, according to an email seen by Reuters on Friday.

An email to Colquhoun's work address bounced back saying he no longer works for the firm and Reuters did not immediately respond to LinkedIn request.

Hill will start his role at Sona Asset Management in October, the source said. He did not immediately respond to a request for comment on LinkedIn. A representative for Cheyne Capital did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

Sona Asset Management, which oversees $13.6 billion, invests in bonds, as well as private credit and collateralised loan obligations, taking both long and short positions. A short trade is a bet that an asset will lose value.

At Cheyne Capital, Hill was responsible for management accounting, budgeting, and financial reporting. He also served on the executive committee, overseeing strategy and planning, the source said.

Before his job at Cheyne, he held roles as head of finance and later CFO at Finisterre Capital.

Sona's departing CFO, Colquhoun, joined the hedge fund manager in June 2018, bringing with him 21 years of experience in fund operations, finance, and infrastructure, according to the firm's website.

Last year, Sona Asset Management's Credit Master Fund returned 18.8%, benefiting from a surge in European companies needing to refinance their debt, Reuters previously reported.

The firm's collateralized loan obligations (CLOs) platform made the top quartile of CLOs ranked by Deutsche Bank in 2024. These did not have specific annual percentage returns mentioned in the letter.

During 2025, Sona Asset Management's assets under management have grown over $3 billion.

data.title: Trump Says U.S. Will Take Nearly 10% Equity Stake in Intel — WSJ
data.description:
By Brian Schwartz, Robbie Whelan and Amrith Ramkumar

President Trump said the government is taking a nearly 10% stake in Intel, capping a two-week frenzy in Washington over the future of the company and fueling speculation about what else might be done to help the troubled chip maker.

Trump said in the Oval Office Friday that the company has agreed to give the government the stake as part of discussions about the company's future and billions of dollars in grants it has received from the 2022 Chips Act.

The agreement came out of a meeting last week between Trump and Intel Chief Executive Lip-Bu Tan, which came days after the president called for Tan to resign over his past ties to China.

Tan made a good impression on the president, who then proposed his latest deal with a private-sector company.

"I said, 'You know what? I think the United States should be given 10% of Intel,'" Trump said while recalling the conversation Friday.

As part of the deal, the government won't be on the board or play a major role in the company's governance, Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said in a CNBC interview earlier this week about the continuing conversations .

Intel has about $8 billion in grants from the Chips Act that could be converted to part of the 9.9% equity stake.

The company is losing about $1 billion a month and needs customers or deals for its chip design and manufacturing businesses to get back on track, industry analysts say.

Intel shares were up about 7% on Friday afternoon.

Write to Brian Schwartz at brian.schwartz@wsj.com, Amrith Ramkumar at amrith.ramkumar@wsj.com and Robbie Whelan at robbie.whelan@wsj.com

data.title: Lucid Is Doing a 1-for-10 Reverse Stock Split. What That Means for Shares. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

Lucid wants a higher stock price. A rare reverse stock split will make it happen.

The electric-vehicle start-up said it "will proceed" with a one-for-10 reverse stock split on Thursday evening. Shares will trade at the new price on Sept. 2.

Lucid stock closed at $2.09 on Thursday, flat on the day. Shares traded as low as $1.98 early Friday until Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell's Jackson Hole speech gave the market a boost. Lucid shares were down 0.5% at $2.07 following his remarks, while the S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 1.6% and 1.9%, respectively.

Traders sometimes see reverse stock splits as a sign that a share price will fall further, just as they see traditional stock splits as a source of upside.

Traditional stock splits are supposed to signal to investors that management sees additional gains in the future. That might be true, but splits are pretty standard for most businesses. Microsoft has split shares nine times, the first coming in 1987.

There are outliers such as Berkshire Hathaway Class A shares, which trade just north of $733,000 each. But in general, investors don't like giant stock prices. The average share price for an S&P 500 stock is about $227.

Lucid isn't in the S&P 500. The one-for-10 split won't get shares near the $227 mark.

As for how shares will trade, the reverse split doesn't reveal much about the company's outlook. Things have been tough for Lucid investors for a while. Shares closed at a record $58.05 in February 2021, according to Nasdaq.

Wall Street was much more optimistic about EV start-ups at that time. Back then, analysts projected 2025 sales of roughly $15 billion from sales of roughly 100,000 EVs, according to FactSet. Lucid is on track to sell about 17,000 cars this year and generate $1.3 billion in sales.

It has simply been harder to sell EVs than Wall Street envisioned. Currently, EVs account for roughly 7% to 8% of new-car sales in the U.S. That is about half the amount in Europe and about one-quarter the level in China.

Lucid and all other EV makers have also had to deal with more competition. In 2021, nine EV models sold more than 10,000 vehicles in the U.S. In 2024, there were more than 30 with that kind of sales volume.

All that has made investors less positive. Through Thursday trading, Lucid shares were down 31% year to date and off 42% over the past 12 months.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

data.title: Dow Gains as Fed Chair Powell Opens Door to Rate Cut — WSJ
data.description:
By Joe Wallace and Krystal Hur

U.S. stocks surged Friday, boosting the Dow to its first record close this year after Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell opened the door for the central bank to cut rates next month.

Speaking in Jackson Hole, Wyo., Powell cited risks that inflation will continue rising and that the labor market will keep weakening. He called that combination unusual and suggested it could prompt the Fed to support economic growth by reducing rates.

"The balance of risks appears to be shifting," Powell said. While labor markets appear to be stable, "it is a curious kind of balance that results from a marked slowing in both the supply of and demand for workers."

Powell said tariff-related costs will continue to accumulate in the economy, but for the first time suggested somewhat greater confidence that the effects of higher goods prices would be relatively short-lived.

The Fed chair has faced intense pressure from the Trump administration to slash borrowing costs. Many investors already expect the Fed to cut rates next month, but had grown less confident in that prediction in recent days, after solid economic data and signs of quickening inflation.

Meanwhile, Canada said it would remove its 25% tariff on about half of the U.S. goods it has targeted since March as the country attempts to reset its trading relationship with the U.S.

On Friday:

Major stock indexes jumped at least 1.5%. The S&P 500 snapped a five-session losing streak, its longest since January. The Dow added 846 points, or 1.9%, to a new record of 45631.74.

Treasury yields fell and the dollar dropped. Traders dialed up bets on a September rate cut.

Bitcoin rose 4% to about $116,600.

Chinese stocks kept flying, taking the CSI 300 to a multiyear high.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: RBLX: Roblox Shares Climb After Bullish Analyst Upgrade
data.description:
Aug 22 - Roblox shares moved higher on Friday after Wolfe Research turned bullish on the online gaming platform. Analyst Shweta Khajuria upgraded the stock to "Buy" from "Hold," setting a $150 price target, which implies notable upside from current levels.

Khajuria cited stronger revenue opportunities tied to regional pricing initiatives and growing advertising contributions. She lifted her fiscal 2026 estimates for bookings and EBITDA by 7% and 13%, respectively, reflecting the additional momentum. According to her note, the company's push into differential pricing and in-app optimizations could unlock new growth levers across geographies.

The analyst also sees Roblox benefiting from what she describes as platform flywheel effects. These include improved search and discovery features, faster content creation, and an expanding pipeline of developer tools. Advertising, while still in its early stages, remains another area of potential.

Khajuria now projects incremental 2026 bookings of $318 million from regional pricing and around $300 million in gross revenue from advertising. Both estimates sit above current Street expectations.

Roblox stock has already gained traction in 2025, and Wall Street is beginning to recognize the company's ability to diversify beyond core gaming revenue.

data.title: Walmart Raises Outlook Despite Tariff Pressures
data.description:
Walmart (WMT, Financials) raised its full-year sales and earnings forecast Thursday, despite acknowledging rising tariff-related costs and one-time expense pressure.

CFO John David Rainey told CNBC the company has selectively passed on tariff costs while absorbing others, with item-level pricing decisions guided by category competitiveness. Tariff-impacted costs are continuing to drift upwards, Rainey said.

Despite inflation and duties, U.S. consumers are still spending. Customer traffic rose 1.5% and average ticket size grew 3.1% year over year. Walmart also said its global ad business grew 46%, bolstered by Vizio the $2.3 billion acquisition completed last year while Walmart Connect rose 31%.

For the first time, Walmart posted a profitable quarter for its e-commerce operations both in the U.S. and globally. Rainey said the segment doubled profitability versus the prior quarter.

The company is managing rising costs by accelerating imports and boosting promotions. It's also attracting wealthier customers and capturing share from weaker rivals like Target, which just announced another quarterly sales decline.

General merchandise, once a weak point during peak inflation, saw a rebound with low-single-digit same-store growth. Clothing and fashion really shined for us, Rainey noted.

Tariff concerns remain. Roughly a third of Walmart's U.S. inventory is imported, with China, Mexico, Vietnam, India, and Canada among key sourcing markets. President Donald Trump criticized Walmart's decision to raise some prices earlier this year, saying the retailer should EAT THE TARIFFS.

Even so, CEO Doug McMillon said on the earnings call that the impact on consumers has been gradual so far, though costs are expected to continue rising through Q3 and Q4 as Walmart replenishes inventory at post-tariff price levels.

data.title: Citibank: Announces $1.75B Redemption of 5.864% Notes Due 2025 and $750M Redemption of Floating Rate Notes Due 2025
CSX and Warren Buffett's BNSF Made News. Why Investors Are Disappointed. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

CSX and Burlington Northern Santa Fe announced a partnership on Friday, sending shares of CSX falling while the market roared higher. Investors were hoping for more.

CSX stock dropped about $3 from its high for the day, leaving the shares 3.4% lower at $34.66.

The S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average were up 1.7% and 2%, respectively, following Fed Chair Jerome Powell's Jackson Hole speech, which the market interpreted as paving the way for a September interest rate cut.

BNSF, which is owned by Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway, and CSX announced a collaboration to bring "seamless, efficient, coast-to-coast [intermodal] solutions." Intermodal refers to freight that travels by both railroad and truck for portions of the shipping journey.

It's a way for shippers to keep costs low. Rail is typically lower cost than trucking.

New services and partnerships are fine, but investors are hoping for merger discussions between the two railroads, following developments in the industry. In July, Union Pacific and Norfolk Southern announced plans to merge. The combination, which will face stiff regulatory hurdles, would create a transcontinental railroad.

Large-scale railroad mergers haven't happened in a generation, partly because the industry is relatively consolidated. But if the Union Pacific-Norfolk merger is allowed, it would make sense for CSX and BNSF to consider combining.

Failing to combine might leave CSX at a competitive disadvantage, versus a larger Union Pacific-Norfolk. That is one reason activist investor Ancora is pushing for CSX to look for merger partners now.

Ancora didn't immediately respond to a request for comment about Friday's announcement.

Investors might be overreacting: The partnership isn't a response to the deal between Union Pacific and Norfolk, which leaves unclear how CSX and BNSF might eventually react. CSX noted that agreements like the BNSF partnership take a while to come together and pointed Barron's to another recent deal it struck with Southeast Mexico Express.

"What it is: a collaboration that creates four new long-haul domestic service offerings to compete with Truckload and, conceptually, a combined Union Pacific/Norfolk Southern transcontinental railroad," wrote Evercore ISI analyst Jonathan Chappell on Friday. "What it isn't: a full replacement of an eventual combination of the two rails."

A merger could still be on the table. He rates CSX shares Buy and has a $39 price target for shares, but cautioned investors that a premium similar to the one Union Pacific has agreed to pay for Norfolk Southern would be in the $36 to $39 range.

"The pullback today renders [CSX stock] more interesting, but we still only see upside to our $39 price target," Chappell wrote.

Overreaction or not, railroad investors will have to contend with merger-induced volatility for months to come.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

data.title: Nvidia CEO Praises TSMC, Says Buying Stock Is 'Very Smart'
data.description:
Nvidia (NVDA, Financials) CEO Jensen Huang praised Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (TSMC, Financials) during a visit to Taipei on Friday, calling it one of the greatest companies in the history of humanity. Huang said anyone investing in TSMC is a very smart person.

The comments came amid speculation that the U.S. government may acquire equity in tech companies receiving federal support through the 2022 CHIPS Act. Huang's trip was intended to thank TSMC for its work on Rubin Nvidia's next-generation AI chip architecture which includes six in-development products such as new CPUs and GPUs.

TSMC has been promised $6.6 billion under the CHIPS Act to help build three advanced chip fabs in Arizona. Though Reuters reported the U.S. is exploring equity stakes in firms like Micron and Samsung, The Wall Street Journal later said Washington has no immediate plans to seek shares in companies like TSMC that are expanding U.S. operations.

TSMC shares are up 6.5% year to date. The foundry giant also recently expanded its U.S. investment to $165 billion.

Huang added that Nvidia is working with Taiwan's government to finalize its new local headquarters, dubbed NVIDIA Constellation, citing rapid employee growth and strong demand across the company's Taiwanese supply chain.

Separately, Huang confirmed Nvidia has paused H20 chip production at several suppliers including Taiwan's Foxconn after China froze domestic purchases of the AI chips due to security concerns. The chips had been expected to return to market after securing a U.S. export license. Huang said Nvidia had responded to Beijing's questions and hopes the issue will be resolved soon.

data.title: The stock market soared following Fed Chair Powell's speech. Why it might just be a 'late-summer rally.'
data.description:
By Joy Wiltermuth

Stocks surged after Federal Reserve Chair Powell left the door open for a September interest-rate cut.

U.S. stocks surged to fresh record highs on Friday, after Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell suggested the labor market may be weakening enough to require help in the form of a September interest-rate cut.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average DJIA surged more than 850 points, or 1.9%, to around 45,653, putting it on pace for its first record finish of the year. The S&P 500 SPX was trading up 1.6%, near its prior record close of 6,468.54 on Aug. 14, while the Nasdaq Composite COMP was up 2%.

While the tone earlier in the week was hesitant, investors on Friday were back buying technology stocks MAGS, as well as small-cap stocks RUT and other rate-sensitive parts of the market that could benefit from lower borrowing costs if the economy holds up.

Before Friday, the S&P 500 fell for five straight days through Thursday, logging its longest losing streak since Jan. 2, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

"Powell gave the market what it wanted it hear," said Keith Lerner, co-chief investment officer at Truist Wealth. "That's the bottom line."

The Fed chair, in his final speech at the Jackson Hole Economic Policy Symposium in Wyoming, said that "downside risks" to the labor market were rising and that a September rate cut by the Fed may be warranted.

Powell also suggested tariffs could still trigger an inflation problem, but he sounded more optimistic than in the past about the possibility of there being only one-time price increases.

Who would benefit from rate cuts

A chief reason for holding rates steady would be to see inflation return closer to the central bank's 2% yearly target, especially since higher prices have begun showing up in economic data as President Trump's tariffs take effect.

A bigger problem, however, for the Fed, markets and households would be if the cost of living becomes entrenched above 3% while layoffs also rise.

Over the last four weeks, there have been signs of rising inflation from the cost of services, said John Velis, Americas macro strategist at BNY. If goods inflation starts going higher, the Fed could end up in a position where it cuts rates as inflation is accelerating above 3%, he noted.

Traders had the odds of a rate cut of 25 basis points in September at 85.2% on Friday, above the roughly 75% chance seen Thursday. Yet there's an August jobs report due and more inflation data still on tap before the Fed's next rate-setting meeting in mid-September.

With that backdrop, Velis said Friday's surge in stocks may be a "late-summer rally" based on low participation that "could get more complicated into the September and October period."

September is typically the worst month for stock-market returns.

Is there still upside?

It's been called one of the least-loved bull markets in memory. So why are stocks still climbing despite the uncertainty?

"What happens in a bull market is the surprises are often to the upside," Lerner said. Given that dynamic, he sees the broadening trade into rate-sensitive parts of the equity market as having more room to continue.

Still, there' plenty of hesitancy around stocks at fresh highs. "The market is richly priced, as it was yesterday," said Ashley Weeks, wealth strategist at TD Wealth. "So, just because it's rich, it does not mean it's not going higher. But the key is long-term focus."

For consumers, a rate cut of 25 basis points wouldn't guarantee lower long-term interest rates for someone looking to finance a car or a home. That could be true even if the Fed cuts rates by 75 basis points this year, as TD Wealth economists expect.

"The Fed can pretty well directly impact short-term lending rates," Weeks said. "But things like mortgages are more tied to the 10-year Treasury, which is a byproduct of the free market.

"From our perspective, it's entirely possible the Fed could start lowering short-term rates, and yet we would see mortgage rates stay where they are, or even increase," he added.

What the market is saying

Inflation concerns appeared to be taking a back seat to worries about the jobs market.

The 10-year Treasury yield BX:TMUBMUSD10Y was tumbling Friday, down 7 basis points to 4.26%. Gold prices (GC00) jumped, while the ICE U.S. Dollar Index DXY fell against a basket of rival currencies and Wall Street's "fear gauge," the Cboe Volatility Index VIX, dropped.

Bret Barker, co-head of global rates at TCW, noted that the five-year breakeven inflation rate, which the Fed watches closely to help gauge inflation expectations, was steady Friday. Over the longer term, inflation "looks well anchored," he said.

The U.S. economy has had a very good run in the wake of the pandemic, Barker said. Still, his team thinks investors remain too optimistic about a soft landing and the Fed having the luxury of cutting rates gradually.

"That sounds good," Barker said. "But if unemployment rises, that's not good for risk assets."

-Joy Wiltermuth

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: FLEXSTEEL INDUSTRIES INC SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Flexsteel Industries Inc., a leading manufacturer in the furniture industry, has released its annual Form 10-K report, detailing significant financial growth, strategic initiatives, and operational highlights for the fiscal year ending June 30, 2025. The report underscores the company's commitment to enhancing its manufacturing capabilities, optimizing its supply chain, and maintaining financial agility amidst a competitive market landscape.

Financial Highlights

  • Net sales: $441.1 million, an increase of 6.9% from the prior year driven by unit volume in soft seating products.
  • Gross profit: $97.9 million, reflecting a gross margin of 22.2%, up from 21.1% in the prior year due to fixed cost leverage and supply chain cost savings.
  • Operating income: $26.6 million, an increase from $17.1 million in the prior year, despite a $14.1 million impairment charge related to the Mexicali facility.
  • Net income: $20.2 million, a significant increase from $10.5 million in the prior year, aided by gains on asset sales.
  • Diluted EPS: $3.55, up from $1.91 in the prior year, reflecting improved profitability and operational efficiency.

Business Highlights

  • Manufacturing and Offshore Sourcing: Flexsteel Industries operates manufacturing facilities in Juarez, Mexico, which are integral to its product offerings and distribution strategy. The company focuses on smaller, more frequent product runs and integrates manufactured products with imported components to offer a wide range of price points and styles.
  • Competition: The furniture industry is highly competitive, with numerous U.S. and foreign manufacturers. Flexsteel competes on style, quality, comfort, price, delivery, service, and durability, leveraging its patented Blue Steel Spring and experienced teams as competitive advantages.
  • Foreign Operations: Flexsteel has minimal export sales and operates three manufacturing facilities in Juarez, Mexico, with plans to sublease a facility in Mexicali until demand increases. The company employs approximately 1,000 people in Mexico.
  • Customer Backlog: As of June 30, 2025, the customer order backlog was approximately $66.5 million, showing an increase from $59.5 million in 2024 and $49.7 million in 2023.
  • Raw Materials: The company uses various materials such as wood, fabric, leather, and steel, sourced from multiple suppliers. While materials are generally available, supply-chain disruptions could impact availability.
  • Environmental Matters: Flexsteel is committed to sustainable business practices and complies with environmental laws regarding product content and industrial waste.
  • Trademarks and Patents: The company owns patents for its Blue Steel Spring and convertible beds, along with other patents and trademarks related to its furniture products.
  • Employees: As of June 30, 2025, Flexsteel employed approximately 1,400 people, with a small number covered by collective bargaining agreements.
  • Future Outlook: For fiscal 2026, Flexsteel aims to maintain financial agility, build supply chain resiliency, focus on operational excellence, and enhance digital capabilities to support long-term growth in retail and e-commerce channels.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Manufacturing Network Optimization: The company has focused on optimizing its manufacturing network, including the closure of the Dublin, Georgia facility, and the sale of ancillary buildings in Huntingburg, Indiana. These actions are part of a broader strategy to streamline operations and improve efficiency.
  • Supply Chain Resilience and Digital Capabilities: Flexsteel is enhancing its supply chain resilience and digital capabilities to support long-term growth in both retail and e-commerce channels.
  • Capital Management: The company has actively managed its capital through a series of strategic actions, including the reduction of its revolving line of credit from $85 million to $55 million to better align with current borrowing needs. It has also engaged in share repurchase programs, with $30 million remaining under the approved program as of June 30, 2025. The company declared cash dividends of $0.71 per share for the fiscal year 2025, reflecting a commitment to returning value to shareholders. Additionally, the company has maintained a strong liquidity position with a significant increase in cash and cash equivalents, ending the year with $40 million.
  • Future Outlook: Looking ahead to fiscal 2026, the company plans to remain financially agile with strong liquidity, continue building its foundation for profitable long-term growth, and focus on operational excellence. The company is also assessing the impact of new trade policies and the One Big Beautiful Bill Act on its operations and tax strategies, with an emphasis on mitigating potential impacts through supply chain adjustments and pricing actions.

Challenges and Risks

  • Trade Policy and Tariffs: Recent executive orders have imposed a 20% tariff on goods from Vietnam, which could materially impact net sales, cost of goods sold, and cash flow. The dynamic nature of trade negotiations adds uncertainty to future operations.
  • Inflation and Currency Fluctuations: Rising costs in raw materials, labor, and transportation, along with foreign currency fluctuations, particularly between the U.S. dollar and the Mexican Peso, could adversely affect profitability.
  • Economic Downturns: As home furnishings are deferrable purchases, prolonged economic downturns could significantly impact consumer demand and the company's sales.
  • Supply Chain Management: The global interdependence of the supply chain is vulnerable to disruptions from tariffs, geopolitical pressures, and logistical challenges, which could affect the company's ability to meet customer demands.
  • Public Health Events: Events like the COVID-19 pandemic could disrupt operations, supply chains, and consumer demand, impacting financial performance.
  • Cybersecurity: The shift to hybrid work models increases vulnerability to cyber attacks, which could disrupt operations and damage the company's reputation.
  • Management Strategies: Management highlights the impact of tariffs on goods from Vietnam and the potential for further trade policy changes as significant risks. The company is exploring supply chain adjustments and pricing strategies to mitigate these impacts. Additionally, the impairment of the Mexicali facility lease due to trade policy changes underscores the risk of geopolitical factors on asset utilization and financial results.
  • Market Risks: The company faces market risks from foreign currency fluctuations, particularly with the Mexican Peso, which could increase manufacturing costs and impact earnings. Interest rate changes also pose a risk, although the company currently has no outstanding balance on its line of credit.

SEC Filing: FLEXSTEEL INDUSTRIES INC [ FLXS ] - 10-K - Aug. 22, 2025

data.title: Ibovespa Surges to Over 1-Month High
data.description:
The Ibovespa surged 2.6% to close at 137,968 on Friday, reaching over one-month highs propelled by a global risk rally after Fed Chair Jerome Powell’s Jackson Hole remarks raised the odds of a September rate cut amid signs of a cooling US labor market.

Major banks led the advance with Banco do Brasil, Banco Santander, Itaúsa and Bradesco rising between 2.9% and 4.4%, while hopes for easier financing also buoyed commodity names and lifted Vale about 2.5%.

For the week the São Paulo exchange posted a gain of roughly 1.2%.

data.title: CERO THERAPEUTICS HOLDINGS, INC. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
CERO Therapeutics Holdings, Inc., a biotechnology company focused on developing innovative therapies for cancer treatment, has released its Form 10-Q report for the second quarter of 2025. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance and significant business developments during this period.

Financial Highlights

Total Operating Expenses: $4.72 million for the three months ended June 30, 2025, reflecting a decrease from $5.15 million for the same period in 2024, indicating improved cost management.

Loss from Operations: $(4.72) million for the three months ended June 30, 2025, compared to $(5.15) million for the same period in 2024, showing a reduction in operating losses.

Net Loss: $(5.42) million for the three months ended June 30, 2025, compared to $(2.45) million for the same period in 2024, highlighting increased losses due to other expenses.

Net Loss Attributable to Common Shareholders: $(30.12) million for the three months ended June 30, 2025, significantly higher than $(2.45) million for the same period in 2024, primarily due to deemed dividends.

Net Loss Per Common Share: $(61.71) for the three months ended June 30, 2025, compared to $(302.32) for the same period in 2024, reflecting the impact of increased shares outstanding.

Business Highlights

New Product Launches: CERO Therapeutics Holdings, Inc. announced the first patient was dosed with its lead compound, CER-1236, in a Phase 1/1b trial for acute myelogenous leukemia (AML) in May 2025. This marks a significant milestone in the company's clinical development efforts.

Clinical Development Progress: The company completed the initial evaluation of the first patient in its Phase 1/1b trial without any dose-limiting toxicity and dosed the second AML patient in July 2025. This progress indicates a positive trajectory in the clinical trial phase.

Regulatory Milestones: In March 2025, the FDA accepted the company's second Investigational New Drug (IND) application for the investigation of CER-T cell therapy in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) and ovarian cancer, expanding the potential therapeutic applications of their technology.

Future Outlook: The company is focused on continued product development, including clinical development, to support regulatory approval and subsequent product commercialization. However, the company acknowledges the need for additional funding to maintain operations and continue R&D activities.

Operational Challenges: The company faces risks inherent in an early-stage biotechnology company, including limited management resources, intense competition, and dependence on the availability of cash to sustain operations. These factors could materially affect the company's operating results.

Geographical Performance: The company is based in South San Francisco, California, and is actively engaged in clinical trials and regulatory activities in the United States, as evidenced by the FDA's involvement in their IND applications.

Business Combination: The merger with Phoenix Biotech Acquisition Corp. was completed in February 2024, resulting in CERO Therapeutics Holdings, Inc. as the surviving entity. This strategic move was aimed at enhancing the company's operational capabilities and financial position.

SEC Filing: CERO THERAPEUTICS HOLDINGS, INC. [ CERO ] - 10-Q - Aug. 22, 2025

data.title: enCore Energy Corp. Completes Upsized $115 Million Offering of Senior Convertible Notes
data.description:
NASDAQ:EU

TSXV:EU

www.encoreuranium.com

DALLAS, Aug. 22, 2025 /PRNewswire/ - enCore Energy Corp. (the "Company" or "enCore"), America's Clean Energy Company(TM), announced today it has closed its previously announced offering of $115 million aggregate principal amount of 5.50% Convertible Senior Notes due 2030 (the "Convertible Notes"), which includes the upsized offering of $100 million and the exercise in full of the $15 million option granted to the initial purchasers of the Convertible Notes.

The initial conversion rate for the Convertible Notes is 303.9976 common shares per $1,000 principal amount of notes, which represents an initial conversion price of $3.2895 per common share. The initial conversion price represents a premium of 27.5% over the last reported sale price of $2.58 per common share on August 19, 2025 on The Nasdaq Capital Market. Under certain circumstances, the conversion price may be adjusted. However, the rate will in no event be greater than 387.5968 common shares per $1,000 principal amount of notes (subject to adjustment), which represents a conversion price of approximately $2.58 per common share.

The net proceeds from the offering were approximately $109.8 million, after deducting the initial purchasers' discounts and commissions and estimated offering expenses. The Company used $11.5 million of the net proceeds from the Convertible Notes offering to pay the cost of entering into capped call transactions in connection with the Convertible Notes and approximately $10.6 million of the net proceeds from the Convertible Notes offering to repay amounts outstanding under its loan agreement. enCore intends to use the remainder of the net proceeds from the Convertible Notes offering for general corporate purposes.

The Convertible Notes and enCore's common shares issuable upon conversion of the Convertible Notes, if any, have not been and will not be registered under the Securities Act, or any state securities laws, or qualified by way of a prospectus in any province or territory of Canada. As a result, neither the Convertible Notes nor any common shares issuable upon conversion of the Convertible Notes may be offered or sold in the United States except pursuant to an applicable exemption from, or in a transaction not subject to, the registration requirements of the Securities Act and applicable state securities laws, and may not be offered or sold to persons located or resident in Canada until December 23, 2025 except pursuant to an exemption from the prospectus requirements of applicable Canadian securities laws. This news release is neither an offer to sell nor a solicitation of an offer to buy the Convertible Notes or any common shares issuable upon conversion of the Convertible Notes, nor will there be any sale of any securities in any jurisdiction in which such offer, solicitation or sale would be unlawful.

Cantor Fitzgerald & Co. acted as sole bookrunner. Haywood Securities Inc. acted as a co-manager. Sidley Austin LLP and Bennett Jones LLP acted as legal advisors to the initial purchasers. HudsonWest LLC acted as the Company's financial advisor and Winston & Strawn LLP and Morton Law LLP served as the Company's legal advisors.

All references to dollar amounts contained in this press release are expressed in United States dollars.

About enCore Energy Corp.

enCore Energy Corp., America's Clean Energy Company(TM), is committed to providing clean, reliable, and affordable fuel for nuclear energy as the only United States uranium company with multiple Central Processing Plants in operation. The enCore team is led by industry experts with extensive knowledge and experience in all aspects of ISR uranium operations and the nuclear fuel cycle. enCore solely utilizes ISR for uranium extraction, a well-known and proven technology co-developed by the leaders at enCore Energy.

Following upon enCore's demonstrated success in South Texas, future projects in enCore's planned project pipeline include the Dewey-Burdock project in South Dakota and the Gas Hills project in Wyoming. The Company holds other assets including non-core assets and proprietary databases. enCore is committed to working with local communities and indigenous governments to create positive impact from corporate developments.
